input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. The physical twin’s architecture consists of lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, and it serves as the platform for an adaptive cruise control (ACC) system. Its modular hardware and the reliance on resource‑constrained ECUs make it particularly suitable for digital twinning, enabling the separation of computation, sensing, and actuation tasks between the physical and virtual representations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin offers three primary services: 1) **Data Monitoring** – a user interface that displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle data; 2) **Computation Offloading** – the ACC algorithm is migrated from the vehicle’s ECU to cloud-based compute resources, where it determines the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance, and sends the desired speed back to the vehicle for actuation; 3) **Remote Management** – operators can remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands through the UI while maintaining low end‑to‑end latency communication with the vehicle.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a low‑latency communication architecture between the physical twin and the cloud to support real‑time data exchange. The digital twin system comprises: a UI layer that aggregates and visualizes sensor data; a data collection layer that persists vehicle state and generates reports; and middleware that orchestrates offloaded ACC computations on cloud services. Standard automotive protocols are used for control command transmission, enabling lightweight ECU offloading while preserving safety‑critical operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Synchronization between physical and digital components is achieved through a shared clock with an accuracy requirement of 100 ms, in line with safety‑critical vehicular standards. All ROS2 nodes across the Digital Twin Entity and the Physical Twin Entity adhere to this licensing, ensuring computation offloading can occur without exceeding a 100 ms end‑to‑end latency bound. Sensor sampling, model inference, and actuator command cycles therefore operate at a maximum frequency of 10 Hz (one cycle per 100 ms), providing the temporal granularity needed for real‑time vehicle operation.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture comprises three distinct twin instances aligned with the ISO 23247 entities: the Physical Twin Entity (sensors, actuators, preprocessing), the Digital Twin Entity (data management, control logic, model inference), and the User Entity (UI components such as 3D visualization and control panel). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware nodes, yielding a decentralized architecture. Inter‑entity coordination is executed via ROS2 communication primitives (topics, services, actions), with clear scope responsibilities: the Physical Twin handles data acquisition and basic processing (e.g., point cloud clustering and tracking), the Digital Twin manages higher‑level inference and control decisions, and the User Entity presents visualizations and user controls.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation follows an ISO 23247 reference architecture, translated into a distributed ROS2‑based system. Each software component is encapsulated as an individual ROS2 package representing a Feature Element (FE). Entities map the system: the Digital Twin Entity hosts data storage and control FEs, the Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific FEs such as sensor interfaces and general automotive functions (e.g., point cloud clustering & tracking), and the User Entity contains the User Interface FE split into 3D visualization and control panel sub‑components. System‑wide coordination is achieved through ROS2 middleware, with data flows governed by a central Data Storage FE and resource management handled implicitly by the middleware’s Quality‑of‑Service (QoS) settings.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The DT system captures raw sensor streams directly from the physical twin. An on‑board LiDAR produces dense 3‑D point‑cloud data which, alongside vehicle state telemetry (speed, yaw rate, acceleration, steering angle, and actuator status), is forwarded via the vehicle’s internal Ethernet link to a Data Translation component. This component serialises the raw data into ROS2 topics, aligns message types (e.g., PointCloud2, PoseStamped), performs unit conversions and timestamps, and emits them over a DDS‑based ROS2 network. Subsequently, the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component applies a voxel‑grid filter, runs Euclidean clustering to extract bounding‑boxes, and publishes object tracks in a dedicated ROS2 topic (e.g., /object_tracks). All sensor streams are additionally passed through a Data Pre‑Processing FE that validates payload integrity with checksums, synchronises clocks, and aggregates messages before they are forwarded to the Presentation FE, which renders them on a 3‑D Visualization User Interface FE. Inter‑entity communication between the vehicle and the remote edge node occurs over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP; the translated ROS2 messages transit this link to be stored in a Data Storage FE and made available for reporting and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT architecture relies on a ROS2 network for intra‑software component communication, where DDS is the underlying transport protocol providing reliable and timely message passing between components such as the Data Translation FE, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and UI FE. Connectivity across physical and virtual entities is achieved using UDP over a Wi‑Fi network, which transmits the ROS2 topics generated by the translation component to external network devices. No explicit details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms (e.g., TLS, VPN, or authentication) are specified in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system follows ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture). It implements an entity‑based reference architecture comprising four principal entities: Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs) responsible for specific tasks such as data acquisition, control, data transformation, or user interaction. The standard does not mandate specific implementation technologies, but the example from the document cites OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as a possible implementation platform, highlighting support for secure, interoperable, and real‑time data exchange among the entities.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: According to 3GPP Technical Specification 22.185 (v15) – Service requirements for V2X services – the Digital Twin system must apply a clear data‑ownership model in which each data source (e.g., vehicle sensors, roadside units) is registered with an owner identifier.  Consent is managed through a service‑level agreement— the Service Provider presents an explicit opt‑in/opt‑out interface that records the data subject’s consent in a secure evidence log.  The standard requires that any personal or identifiable data be protected in compliance with applicable data‑protection regulations (e.g., GDPR or equivalent local laws) by using pseudonymisation— each vehicle’s identifier is replaced by a temporary pseudonym that changes on a per‑message basis; the pseudonym chain can be revoked only by the network operator.  All data exchanges are governed by data sharing agreements explicitly stated in the SLAs that bind network operators, vehicle manufacturers and third‑party service providers.  The Digital Twin ensures that data retention is limited to the period specified by the respective agreement and that deleted data are irrevocably erased from all storage units.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin leverages both ITS safety and cybersecurity frameworks.  From ISO 26262‑1:2018 (Road vehicles – Functional safety) it implements a full safety life‑cycle: hazard identification, risk assessment via ASIL (A–D) classification, safety goal definition and validation, plus test‑coverage metrics for system, hardware and software.  The system is subjected to fail‑safe mechanisms such as watchdog timers, watchdog‑based reset on counter‑fault detection, and redundancy in critical safety paths.  For cybersecurity, 3GPP TS 22.185 mandates the use of ETSI V2X security architecture: authentication of messages using asymmetric cryptography (ECDSA P‑256) and integrity protection by message authentication codes derived from group keys.  Access control is enforced via role‑based access control for internal services, and encryption of all storage and communication channels is performed with TLS 1.3 in the application layer and DTLS 1.3 for lower‑layer UDP traffic.  Threat mitigation includes certificate revocation lists and periodic re‑keying every 24 hours.  Risk assessment additionally follows a combined functional safety and cybersecurity evaluation model defined in the ISO 26262/ISA‑2144 compatibility matrix, ensuring that safety‑critical requirements are not compromised by cyber‑attacks.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. _A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
